LAW & MOTION, DEPT. 302, AS TO THE OCT-16-2024 HEARING RE: DEFENDANT NOELLE MORENO'S MOTION TO STRIKE AMENDED COMPLAINT, THE COURT ADOPTS ITS TENTATIVE RULING. OFF CALENDAR. THE ORDONEZ DECLARATION FAILS TO SHOW THAT THE PARTIES MET AND CONFERRED IN PERSON, BY TELEPHONE, OR BY VIDEOCONFERENCE IN COMPLIANCE WITH CCP 435.5(A). THE PARTIES ARE ORDERED TO COMPLY WITH THE CODE. THE RESPONSE TO THE AMENDED COMPLAINT IS NOW DUE BY NOVEMBER 15, 2024. JUDGE: RICHARD B. ULMER JR.; CLERK: V. DA FONSECA; NOT REPORTED. (302/RBU)

CASE MANAGEMENT CONFERENCE

CASE MANAGEMENT CONFERENCE
Off Calendar Aug-05-2024 Continued To Oct-09-24 At 10:30 A.M. In Dept 610

MOTION TO STRIKE COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Aug-14-2024 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
No appearances. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANT NOELLE MORENO's MOTION TO STRIKE COMPLAINT.

Grant with leave to amend.

Allegations of punitive damages must be supported by non-conclusory allegations of facts showing the actions complained-of were e.g., malicious, fraudulent, or oppressive. The complaint must have "specific factual allegations showing that defendant's conduct was oppressive, fraudulent, or malicious to support a claim for punitive damages.. Punitive damages may not be pleaded generally." Today's IV, Inc. v. Los Angeles Cnty. Metro. Transportation Auth., 83 Cal. App. 5th 1137, 1193 (2022) (citation omitted). All the allegations in the current complaint are consistent with ordinary negligence; indeed, many of those highlighted by the plaintiff are consistent with no fault at all; e.g., the dog was not on leash, was in a public area, and so on. The sole allegation that suggests intentional action is that the defendants had "the intent to injure members of the public," but that is conclusory: there are no facts from which one might infer such an intent. Nothing else in the complaint distinguishes this case from any case in which any dog is unleashed on a sidewalk, and that, in turn, cannot possibly evidence an intent to injure and justify punitive damages.

Plaintiff's papers suggest that the punitive damages allegations were inserted to prevent the defendant from using a threatened bankruptcy to avoid liability. See the "Revised Opposition" dated July 31, 2024 at 1:26-17; 5:22 ff. Two points: first, a potential bankruptcy is irrelevant to the pending motion to strike. Secondly, an ethical issue may be presented if allegations are unsupportable and only made for an ulterior purpose. Plaintiff's counsel should deliberately evaluate his professional obligations.

Both sides have asked for sanctions; none complied with-among a series of other requirements--the pre-filing notice requirements of CCP 128.7. The requests are denied.

The motion to strike the demand for punitive damages is granted, with leave to amend. A new complaint may be filed and served not later than August 28, 2024.

Counsel for the prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling in copliance with CRC 3.1312

Judge: Curtis E.A. Karnow; Clerk: W. Trupek; Not Reported (302/CEK)
